B9AI105 — Reinforcement Learning — CA2 Artifact Demonstration

Reinforcement Learning for
Autonomous Lunar Landing

Deploying trained RL agents as an interactive web application

DQN Double DQN TD3
Afaq Ahmad (20063247)  ·  Saquib Zakir Husein Pirjade (20079780)  ·  Habib Ullah (20073274)

MSc Artificial Intelligence · Dublin Business School · Dr. Anesu Nyabadza · April 2026

2

Environment Design

LunarLander-v3 — States, Actions, Rewards

State Space (8 variables)

  • x, y — position relative to pad
  • vx, vy — velocity
  • theta — body angle
  • omega — angular velocity
  • Left/right leg ground contact

Reward Signal

  • +100 to +140 for safe landing
  • +10 per leg contact
  • -100 for crashing
  • -0.3 per main engine fire
  • Solved: avg reward >= 200
3

Algorithms Implemented

All built from scratch using PyTorch — no pre-built RL libraries

DQN

Deep Q-Network

  • Q-Network: 8 → 64 → 64 → 4
  • Experience replay (100k buffer)
  • Target network (Polyak, τ=0.005)
  • Epsilon-greedy: 1.0 → 0.01
  • Huber loss + gradient clipping

Double DQN

Reduced Overestimation

  • Online net selects action
  • Target net evaluates Q-value
  • Decouples selection & evaluation
  • Reduces maximisation bias
  • Same architecture as DQN

TD3

Twin Delayed DDPG

  • Actor: 8 → 128 → 128 → 2 (tanh)
  • Twin critics (take min Q)
  • Delayed actor updates (every 2)
  • Target policy smoothing
  • Gaussian noise (σ=0.1)
4

DQN — Live Demo

Discrete control — 4 engine actions

272.5
REWARD
232
STEPS
LANDED
STATUS
69%
SUCCESS RATE

How it works

  • Agent observes 8 state variables
  • Q-network outputs Q-value for each of 4 actions
  • Agent picks action with highest Q-value
  • No exploration during evaluation (ε = 0)
5

Double DQN — Live Demo

Discrete control — reduced Q-value overestimation

287.5
REWARD
314
STEPS
LANDED
STATUS
67%
SUCCESS RATE

Key Difference from DQN

  • Online net picks best action: a* = argmax Q(s', a)
  • Target net evaluates it: Q_target(s', a*)
  • Prevents overestimation of Q-values
  • Converged fastest: episode 681
6

TD3 — Live Demo

Continuous control — 2D thrust vector

269.6
REWARD
233
STEPS
LANDED
STATUS
80%
BEST SUCCESS RATE

TD3 Innovations

  • Twin critics: min of two Q-estimates
  • Delayed actor: updates every 2 critic steps
  • Target smoothing: clipped noise on targets
  • Continuous thrust gives precise landings
7

Training Results

All three algorithms solved the environment

AlgorithmSolved AtEval MeanSuccess
DQNEp. 692196.8869%
Double DQNEp. 681195.1867%
TD3Ep. 763206.8180%

Key Findings

  • TD3 achieved highest success rate (80%)
  • Double DQN converged fastest (ep. 681)
  • Continuous control enables smoother landings
  • Discount factor γ=0.99 was critical
8

Hyperparameter Analysis

Discount factor is the most critical parameter

γ = 0.95 — FAILED

Agent can't see delayed landing reward. 0.95^500 ≈ 0

γ = 0.99 — OPTIMAL

~100 step effective horizon. Enough for approach + landing.

γ = 0.999 — UNSTABLE

Too much weight on distant, noisy future rewards.

Failed Experiments

  • 256 hidden units → overfitting
  • No target network → training collapse
  • LR = 0.01 → catastrophic divergence
9

Ethical Considerations

Following the four pillars of research ethics

Safety in Autonomous Systems

RL agents fail hundreds of times during training. In simulation this is harmless. In reality, each failure has consequences. Safety constraints must override learned policies in dangerous states.

Bias in Reward Design

The reward function encodes human values. Our -0.3 fuel penalty prioritises efficiency over speed. Different rewards produce different behaviours. Who decides what "good" landing means?

Transparency

Neural network policies are black boxes. We cannot explain why the agent fires a specific engine at a specific moment. Safety-critical deployment requires explainability current RL cannot provide.

Sim-to-Real Transfer

Trained in 2D with perfect sensors. Real landing involves 3D dynamics, sensor noise, wind, actuator lag. Deploying without addressing this gap is ethically problematic.

Dual-Use Concerns

Same algorithms for landing spacecraft could be applied to autonomous weapons, drone strikes, or surveillance. General-purpose techniques carry dual-use risks.

Accountability

When a hand-coded controller fails, the cause can be traced. When an RL agent fails, who is responsible? The developer, the reward designer, the deployer? No clear framework exists.

10

Limitations

Theory vs deployed solution

Simulation vs Reality

SimulationReality
2D physics3D with 6 DOF
Perfect sensorsNoisy readings
Instant enginesActuator delay
No windTurbulence
Flat terrainRocky surface

Statistical Limitations

  • Single random seed (42)
  • 100-episode evaluation only
  • No cross-validation of hyperparameters

Theory vs Deployment

  • CA1: research context, offline evaluation
  • CA2: same frozen models in a web app
  • No retraining occurs in the deployed app
  • Rendering adds minor latency
  • Local-only deployment, not cloud

No Safety Guarantees

  • No worst-case performance bounds
  • No formal verification
  • Could fail on unseen states
  • Only 3 of many possible algorithms tested
